output:
# GRIDLOCK and LASA: Treating the Sub-Image Partition of a UI Screen as a Per-Screen Layout-Importance Field instead of a Content-Blind Geometric Constant in High-Resolution UI MLLMs

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Mobile-UI understanding has converged on a dominant production recipe for giving a multimodal LLM (MLLM) the visual acuity that user-interface screens demand. Because UI screens have ELONGATED aspect ratios and contain SMALL objects of interest (icons, single-line text, toggles) -- exactly the property the Ferret-UI introduction names as the reason natural-image MLLMs underperform on screens -- the recipe is: SPLIT the screen into sub-images, ENCODE each sub-image at the vision encoder's native resolution, and CONCATENATE the resulting visual tokens (usually together with a low-resolution global thumbnail) into the LLM context. This split-encode-concatenate pattern is the engine of Ferret-UI [1], which divides each screen into a 1x2 or 2x1 grid of sub-images chosen by aspect ratio ("any resolution"), encodes each with CLIP-ViT-L/14@336 [3], and feeds sub-image tokens plus a global image plus Ferret's region sampler into the LLM. The same pattern -- under names like AnyRes, dynamic high-resolution, shape-adaptive cropping, and N-gridding -- powers its successor Ferret-UI 2 [2] and the broader high-resolution MLLM family: LLaVA-NeXT/LLaVA-1.5 [4], InternVL [5], Qwen-VL [6], UReader [7], Monkey [8], InternLM-XComposer2-4KHD [9], and screenshot-native parsers Pix2Struct [15] and ScreenAI [16]. Empirically the recipe works: tiling recovers the small-element signal that a single resized global image destroys, and it is the foundation for UI agents that must read and act on dense screens [10, 11, 17, 18]. The whole value proposition is that high-resolution detail survives encoding -- one elongated screen is decomposed into encoder-sized pieces so that a 16-pixel icon is no longer sub-patch noise.

The silent assumption underneath this recipe is HOW the screen is split: the partition is chosen by a GEOMETRIC rule. Ferret-UI picks 1x2 vs 2x1 by aspect ratio and cuts at the MIDLINE; Ferret-UI 2, LLaVA-NeXT, InternVL, and UReader pick a grid SHAPE/COUNT (n_r x n_c) to best match resolution and aspect ratio, then cut into EQUAL rectangular tiles [2, 4, 5, 7]. Two quantities are therefore treated as GLOBAL CONSTANTS, identical for every screen: (a) WHERE the cuts fall (content-independent geometric midlines), and (b) HOW the encoder/token budget is SPREAD (uniformly across equal tiles). This implicitly asserts that the best place to cut a screen and the best place to spend resolution are INDEPENDENT OF SCREEN CONTENT. But UI layout is the opposite of uniform: it is highly STRUCTURED -- elements cluster into toolbars, lists, keyboards, status bars, and navigation rails separated by whitespace gutters, and element sizes span orders of magnitude (a full-width banner next to a 16-pixel glyph). A geometric grid is blind to all of it.

This is the opening this work targets: a measured-but-not-fed-back gap that is, strikingly, even more acute here than in the data-augmentation literature, because the signal is sitting in the same pipeline. The UI element bounding boxes that the data pipeline uses to CREATE the referring and grounding labels (widget classification, icon recognition, OCR, find-widget, widget-listing) [1] -- the very layout of the screen -- are NOT consulted when the partition is chosen. The partition ignores the layout that the labels are literally built from. Positioned against prior art, the high-resolution tiling lines [1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9] never make the partition content-aware in this sense; shape-adaptive cropping [7] and pix2struct-style flexible patching [15, 16] adapt the grid to ASPECT RATIO (still uniform geometric tiles); visual token reduction methods such as Token Merging [21] act AFTER encoding (they can merge redundant tokens but cannot add resolution to a region that was never encoded finely); and content-aware image resizing in graphics (seam carving [20]) operates on raw pixels for display, with no notion of an MLLM token budget, grounding-coordinate consistency, or an exact reduction to a geometric baseline. Nothing connects the freely available UI layout signal to the partition decision, and that disconnect is what we name and close.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap GRIDLOCK (Geometric-gRId Imposed Decomposition, Layout-Oblivious and Cutting through Konstituent widgets). The high-resolution recipe defines a partitioning rule that places cuts on geometric midlines and spreads the token budget uniformly, implicitly asserting that screen LAYOUT-IMPORTANCE is a CONSTANT field. GRIDLOCK states that layout-importance is in truth a HETEROGENEOUS per-screen FIELD rho: [0,W] x [0,H] -> [0,1], where rho(x,y) is large where small, dense, interaction-relevant UI elements concentrate and near zero in whitespace gutters and decorative backgrounds. Formally, the deployed partition P_geo (equal tiles, midline cuts) is merely the rho == const special case of a layout-conditioned partition P(rho); whenever the realized field is non-uniform, P_geo cuts and allocates in the wrong places.

The defining property of GRIDLOCK is that the harm is SILENT under pooled accuracy and STRUCTURED in where it strikes. It has two mechanisms. MECHANISM 1, SEAM FRAGMENTATION: a geometric cut can pass THROUGH a UI element -- a wide button, a text line, a row of icons -- so that the element is split across two INDEPENDENTLY-encoded sub-images. Neither sub-image contains the whole element, each sub-image's positional frame resets at its own origin, and the cross-sub-image relationship is weakened. Referring on a fragmented widget (classify it, OCR it) and grounding a box that straddles the seam both degrade; this is the same class of tile-boundary artifact documented across the dynamic-tiling literature [4, 5, 7, 8]. MECHANISM 2, RESOLUTION MISALLOCATION: uniform tiling gives a blank-background tile the SAME encoder and token budget as a dense keyboard, list, or status bar, so small elements in dense regions stay under-resolved -- the exact signal anyres was built to recover -- while sparse tiles burn tokens on nothing. The harm CONCENTRATES on small elements, dense regions, and seam-adjacent elements -- precisely Ferret-UI's hardest cases and precisely the regime that the high-resolution GUI-grounding benchmark ScreenSpot-Pro [12] shows is catastrophic today (average target ~0.07% of screen area; best 2025 grounding models near ~18.9% accuracy). It is a MULTIPLIER, because the same content-blind grid is applied to every screen and every platform [2]. And it is INVISIBLE under a pooled mean, because small-element and seam-adjacent cases are a minority of the evaluation mass (metric blindness). The harm VANISHES if and only if the field is constant, rho == const: when a screen really is layout-uniform (a single full-bleed image or video), the geometric partition is already right and there is nothing to gain. The gap exists exactly because UI layout is non-constant.

We introduce LASA (Layout-Aware Sub-image Allocation), which promotes the partition from a geometric constant to a function of the field and acts on it with two ORTHOGONAL knobs. The field is estimated FOR FREE (or at low, reported overhead) from signals the pipeline already produces: (e1) saliency / edge-energy read off the GLOBAL THUMBNAIL feature map that Ferret-UI already computes on the global-image pass -- a genuine byproduct, no extra forward pass; (e2) a class-agnostic UI element proposal from the pipeline's existing detector (or a lightweight one), reused from the same component that already proposes boxes for label creation; and (e3) accessibility / view-hierarchy boxes when available at runtime (free on Android via the view hierarchy [13], and the canonical source of the training labels themselves). The raw estimate rho_hat is passed through a small held-out CALIBRATION map c(.) -- fit by isotonic or Platt scaling [19] on a PROBE set where per-region marginal accuracy-vs-resolution IS measured -- yielding rho_cal = c(rho_hat), anchored so that c(const) = const. KNOB1 (SEAM placement): the cut positions are moved from equal spacing to the LOW-density VALLEYS (gutters) of the marginal density profiles rho_x, rho_y, chosen by a cheap 1-D dynamic program that minimizes the element mass crossed by cuts subject to the encoder's tile aspect/size constraints. KNOB2 (BUDGET allocation): the number of tiles (hence the encoded resolution) assigned to a region R is made proportional to the integrated field there, n_R ~ integral_R rho_cal, under a FIXED total tile/token budget B -- dense regions are recursively sub-gridded, sparse regions stay coarse. A coordinate-frame RAIL makes this safe for grounding: every tile T carries an explicit (origin o_T, scale s_T) embedding, and all grounding coordinates are decoded into one GLOBAL screen frame regardless of how tiles were cut or scaled. The LASA partition is:

  P_LASA(rho_cal) = { tiles whose cut positions = DP-valleys(rho_cal) and whose per-region counts ~ integral rho_cal, under budget B, each tagged with (o_T, s_T) }.

The decisive design property is the EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee [provable-by-construction]. The collapse is a DESIGN SWITCH that disables estimation, not a claim that layout is uniform: set rho_hat == const (disable estimation), so rho_cal = c(const) = const; then the DP valleys of a flat profile are equal-spaced cuts, the budget allocation integral_R const is uniform, and every tile's (o_T, s_T) reduces to the geometric baseline's implicit frame. Tile-for-tile, P_LASA == P_geo, producing the IDENTICAL token multiset and the IDENTICAL coordinate frame, so the entire model's output is unchanged. The exactness budget E_collapse = max over tiles of corner-pixel deviation between P_LASA and P_geo at rho == const is required to be 0 px with an identical token multiset, enforced by a CI-gateable build check (E_collapse = 0 is an implementation invariant, not a performance claim). This is a strict, safe generalization: zero regression at the identity default, on top of any existing tiling MLLM.

The novelty wedge is four clauses that, taken together, no prior line covers more than two of: C1 -- the sub-image partition is modeled as a per-screen LAYOUT-IMPORTANCE FIELD rho(x,y), not a content-blind geometric constant (geometry -> field promotion); C2 -- the field is estimated FOR FREE / low-overhead from signals the pipeline already produces (thumbnail saliency + element proposals + view hierarchy), with NO extra labels, passed through a small held-out calibration map c(.); C3 -- TWO orthogonal knobs act on the SAME calibrated field, a SEAM-placement knob (where to cut) and a BUDGET-allocation knob (how much resolution per region); C4 -- an EXACT-COLLAPSE strict generalization, pixel- and token-exact (E_collapse = 0), back to geometric anyres / N-gridding, PLUS a coordinate-frame rail that keeps grounding coordinates consistent under non-uniform tiling (the correctness condition prior tiling work never needed because its tiles were always uniform).

BOUNDARY RECOVERIES (named baseline => exact special point of the LASA field) [provable-by-construction]:

  R1 Global-only (no split): budget B = 1 tile => single resized global image (the low-res baseline anyres was meant to beat).
  R2 Fixed 1x2 / 2x1 anyres (Ferret-UI [1]): rho == const, B = 2 => geometric midline split, uniform budget => P_LASA == P_geo. The deployed baseline and the collapse target.
  R3 Resolution-driven uniform N-gridding (Ferret-UI 2 [2] / LLaVA-NeXT [4] / InternVL [5] / UReader [7]): rho == const, B = N => geometric N-tile grid, uniform budget.
  R4 Seam-only (KNOB1 on, KNOB2 off): content-aware cut positions but uniform per-region budget (isolates fragmentation repair).
  R5 Budget-only (KNOB2 on, KNOB1 off): geometric cut positions but density-proportional resolution (isolates misallocation repair).
  R6 Oracle layout (rho from ground-truth element boxes / view hierarchy at test time): the field ceiling.

Load-bearing symbols: I screen image of size W x H; rho(x,y) = layout-importance field in [0,1]; rho_x, rho_y marginal density profiles; rho_hat free estimate; c(.) calibration map; rho_cal = c(rho_hat); P_geo geometric (equal-tile, midline) partition; P_LASA layout-aware partition; B total tile/token budget; n_R tiles allocated to region R; T a tile with origin o_T and scale s_T; the coordinate-frame rail = the (o_T, s_T) tagging that keeps grounding in one global frame; E_collapse exact-collapse budget (px / token exactness); epsilon do-no-harm non-inferiority margin; frag_rate = fraction of ground-truth elements crossed by a cut; identity default = disable estimation (rho_hat == const => P_LASA == P_geo).

BRAINSTORMING SUMMARY (ideas considered, and why LASA was selected). We surveyed four candidate gaps in the Ferret-UI program: (i) content-aware partitioning of the high-resolution tiling (chosen); (ii) calibrated grounding-coordinate UNCERTAINTY for safe tapping in UI agents; (iii) annotation-vs-pixel consistency of view-hierarchy grounding labels (occluded/stale/decorative boxes as mislabeled targets); and (iv) single-screen -> stateful multi-step UI understanding. Idea (i) was selected because it (a) attacks Ferret-UI's headline architectural contribution (the any-resolution sub-image scheme) head-on, (b) admits the same rigorous template that the field rewards -- constant -> field, free estimation, two orthogonal knobs, exact collapse, boundary recoveries -- and (c) has a uniquely clean FREE signal in the UI domain (element boxes already in the pipeline) that natural-image saliency lacks, with a directly measurable mechanism metric (frag_rate). Ideas (ii)-(iv) are noted as complementary future directions.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does promoting the sub-image partition of a UI screen from a content-blind geometric constant to a per-screen layout-importance field rho(x,y) -- estimated for free from the thumbnail saliency, element proposals, and view-hierarchy boxes the pipeline already produces, calibrated on a small probe, and used to place cut seams on low-density gutters (SEAM) and to allocate per-region resolution proportional to element density (BUDGET) under a fixed token budget -- improve referring and grounding accuracy on small and seam-adjacent UI elements, at equal token cost and without regressing at the identity default, relative to geometric any-resolution tiling?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 (the status quo it disrupts: a content-blind geometric partition). The dominant recipe for high-resolution UI understanding splits a screen into sub-images on a geometric grid -- midline cuts and equal tiles chosen by aspect ratio or resolution -- and encodes each tile at native resolution. This is the engine of Ferret-UI [1] and its whole neighborhood (Ferret-UI 2 [2], LLaVA-NeXT [4], InternVL [5], UReader [7], Monkey [8]). That partition silently asserts a GLOBAL CONSTANT: layout-importance is uniform, so cuts and resolution can be placed geometrically. LASA disrupts exactly this unexamined constant: GRIDLOCK asserts rho is a heterogeneous per-screen field, and the deployed partition P_geo is merely the rho == const special case of P(rho). The community has scaled tiling resolution (more tiles, bigger encoders) without ever questioning WHERE the tiles are placed.

P2 (the silent harm: fragmented and under-resolved elements, multiplied, concentrated, invisible). A geometric cut can slice a widget in half (SEAM FRAGMENTATION) and uniform tiling can starve a dense keyboard while feeding a blank background (RESOLUTION MISALLOCATION). Both are first-order perception failures, and they land where UI tasks are hardest: small elements, dense regions, seam-adjacent elements -- the documented weak spot of every grounding model on high-resolution screens [12]. The grid is a MULTIPLIER (the same content-blind rule hits every screen and platform [2]), and the harm is INVISIBLE under pooled accuracy because those cases are a minority of the eval mass (metric blindness). The pipeline already has every detection signal -- a thumbnail feature map, element proposals, the view hierarchy [13] that the labels are built from -- yet feeds none of it into the partition: the measured-but-not-fed-back gap, here in its sharpest form.

P3 (why it advances knowledge: an unmeasured architectural assumption becomes a calibrated, inspectable field). The contribution is epistemic before it is empirical. LASA turns the unmeasured assumption (layout-importance is uniform) into a calibrated, inspectable per-screen quantity rho_cal = c(rho_hat), and exposes a direct MECHANISM metric -- frag_rate, the fraction of ground-truth elements a cut crosses -- that no tiling paper currently reports. This reframes the community's question from "how many tiles / how big an encoder?" (a resolution-scaling race) to "WHERE is each screen information-dense, and where may we cut?" -- a per-screen, stratifiable question. It yields a reusable LAYOUT-FIELD-QUALITY protocol (AUROC / ECE of rho_cal against oracle element density [19], plus frag_rate) that any future tiling scheme can be audited with, independent of the backbone it feeds. Naming rho and measuring frag_rate is the durable knowledge; the two knobs are the first thing one does once the field exists.

P4 (practical payoff: free signal, backbone-agnostic, drop-in, risk-free adoption). The estimator signals are cheap: thumbnail saliency (e1) is a byproduct of the global-image pass Ferret-UI already runs, element proposals (e2) reuse the pipeline's detector, and the view hierarchy (e3) is free on Android [13]; the seam solver is a 1-D dynamic program over O(W+H) candidate cuts. LASA is therefore backbone-agnostic (any tiling MLLM: Ferret-UI [1], Ferret-UI 2 [2], LLaVA-NeXT [4], InternVL [5]) and drops in as a partition pre-processor. Adoption is risk-free by construction: disabling estimation (set rho_hat == const) reproduces geometric anyres tile-for-tile with E_collapse = 0, removing the usual barrier to touching a working pipeline; a do-no-harm non-inferiority margin epsilon underwrites the near-identity regime. Crucially, LASA buys accuracy at FIXED tokens (or equal accuracy at FEWER tokens), which matters because UI screens are token-hungry and on-device UI assistants are compute-constrained.

P5 (the stakes: agents act on small targets; accessibility and reliability ride on grounding). UI understanding is a building block for accessibility, multi-step navigation, and app testing [1, 11, 17, 18], and downstream agents convert a grounded box into a TAP. A fragmented or under-resolved element becomes a wrong coordinate and a wrong action; on professional high-resolution screens this is where today's models collapse [12]. LASA targets the dominant failure surface of the line -- small-element grounding on dense screens -- at the metric the agent community cares about (click accuracy), for free, with a provable safety net. Because the same content-blind grid is reused across platforms in the Ferret-UI 2 universal-UI program [2], fixing the partition compounds across iPhone, Android, iPad, web, and TV rather than helping one device.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 COORDINATE-FRAME CONSISTENCY [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Moving seams and using non-uniform per-region resolution changes the local-to-global pixel mapping of every tile. NAIVE FAILS: concatenating tiles with per-tile normalized coordinates (the convention that works for a uniform grid) makes the LLM emit grounding boxes in an ambiguous frame, so localization collapses precisely when tiles stop being equal. REPRESENTATIONAL because the fix is a structural choice about how coordinates are represented. MITIGATION: tag every tile with an explicit (o_T, s_T) and train/decode all grounding in one global (W,H) frame; the EXACT-COLLAPSE check verifies the frame is identical to the baseline at rho == const, so the rail cannot silently regress R2/R3.

O2 ESTIMATOR CIRCULARITY: SALIENCY != TASK-IMPORTANCE [REPRESENTATIONAL]. The free inference-time channel e1 reads the model's own thumbnail saliency, which concentrates where the model ALREADY attends -- under-resolving the small elements it currently MISSES, which are exactly the ones we want to up-resolve. NAIVE FAILS: a self-saliency field re-tiles to confirm the model's current competence and leaves its blind spots blind. REPRESENTATIONAL because it is a choice of WHERE the importance signal comes from. MITIGATION: anchor rho on NON-model channels (element proposals e2, view hierarchy e3); score e1 with a frozen/cross-fit saliency teacher, never the live model; calibrate against held-out element-detection labels. Falsified by the PLACEBO rung; bounded by the ORACLE rung (R6).

O3 CALIBRATION WITHOUT PER-SCREEN BUDGET LABELS [REPRESENTATIONAL]. KNOB2 needs rho_cal to predict MARGINAL ACCURACY GAIN PER UNIT RESOLUTION, not raw element density; a text-dense but easy region (large readable text) should not steal budget from a tiny-icon region. NAIVE FAILS: raw density over-allocates to dense-but-easy regions. REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces the calibration map c(.) into the architecture -- rho_hat is never used directly; only rho_cal drives SEAM and BUDGET. MITIGATION: fit c(.) on a small held-out probe where per-region accuracy-vs-resolution is actually measured (sweep tile resolution, observe accuracy), via isotonic / Platt [19]; anchor c(const) = const so EXACT-COLLAPSE holds; report ECE(rho_cal) << ECE(rho_hat).

O4 TOKEN-BUDGET CONFOUND [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Finer tiling means more tokens means more accuracy regardless of placement, so a gain could be "just more compute," not "better placement." NAIVE FAILS: an unmatched comparison cannot separate the claim (measuring layout) from the confound (spending tokens). REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces a control INTO the design: the total token budget B is FIXED and reported with every number. MITIGATION: a token-matched PLACEBO that allocates the SAME budget B by a random (or geometric) field; PASS requires LASA > placebo at equal B by more than seed noise.

O5 SEAM-OBJECTIVE DISCRETENESS. Choosing cut positions is a combinatorial placement problem under encoder aspect/size constraints. NAIVE FAILS: greedy "cut at the single deepest valley" can violate tile-shape limits or strand a dense region in one oversized tile. MITIGATION: a 1-D dynamic program over a discretized set of admissible cuts that minimizes crossed element mass subject to min/max tile aspect and a fixed cut count, with an optional soft relaxation; cost is O(W+H), reported.

O6 INFERENCE OVERHEAD AND THE VIEW-HIERARCHY-FREE CASE. rho and the seam solve must be cheap relative to the ViT/LLM, and at deployment the view hierarchy (e3) may be unavailable (iOS, web canvas, games). NAIVE FAILS: a field that needs an expensive extra detector, or that degrades when e3 is missing, is not deployable. MITIGATION: compute rho from the already-available thumbnail features (e1) plus a lightweight proposal (e2), with e3 as a free bonus when present; per-channel reliability weighting so the field never goes blind when a channel drops; report wall-clock overhead (target a few percent of one forward pass).

O7 METRIC BLINDNESS [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Pooled accuracy averages over many elements; gains that concentrate on small and seam-adjacent elements barely move the mean. NAIVE FAILS: reporting only the pooled mean HIDES both the harm (GRIDLOCK) and the fix (LASA). REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces the EVALUATION's structure -- the primary readout must be the stratified cells, since the method's value is the GRADIENT of lift across strata. MITIGATION: stratified accuracy as PRIMARY, cells = {element size small/med/large} x {seam-adjacent yes/no} x {layout density low/med/high}; pooled reported only for non-inferiority.

WHY IT IS NONETHELESS TRACTABLE. Every obstacle is matched by a structural feature already in the frame: circularity (O2) is fenced by non-model channels (e2, e3) and a frozen/cross-fit teacher; the missing-budget-label problem (O3) is solved by a tiny calibrated probe; the token confound (O4) and metric blindness (O7) are neutralized by the fixed budget B, the token-matched PLACEBO, and the mandated stratified readout; coordinate consistency (O1) and the missing-view-hierarchy case (O6) degrade gracefully because the field is multi-channel and the rail is explicit. Above all, the EXACT-COLLAPSE identity (rho_hat == const => P_LASA == P_geo, E_collapse = 0) guarantees LASA does no harm relative to the deployed geometric baseline -- the downside is pinned to zero by construction, so the entire research risk is on the UPSIDE.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior art falls into five families; for each we give representative work, what it does, the precise delta LASA adds, and the boundary recovery (R2-R6) it maps to.

(1) High-resolution UI / document MLLMs with geometric tiling -- Ferret-UI [1], Ferret-UI 2 [2], LLaVA-NeXT [4], InternVL [5], Monkey [8], InternLM-XComposer2-4KHD [9]. WHAT: split the screen into a grid chosen by aspect ratio / resolution and encode equal tiles. DELTA: none makes cut placement or per-region budget a function of layout; they assert rho == const. LASA adds the full wedge C1 (geometry -> field), C2 (free estimator + calibration), C3 (two orthogonal knobs), C4 (exact collapse + coordinate rail). These are precisely R2 (B = 2) and R3 (B = N): rho == const => P_LASA == P_geo.

(2) Aspect-ratio-adaptive cropping / flexible patching -- UReader shape-adaptive cropping [7], Pix2Struct variable-resolution patches [15], ScreenAI flexible patching [16]. WHAT: choose the grid SHAPE to match the image's aspect ratio so tiles are not distorted. DELTA: adaptation is to global geometry, not to internal CONTENT -- tiles remain uniform and cuts remain content-blind, so fragmentation and misallocation persist. LASA conditions on rho INSIDE the screen, not just its outer shape; these methods are still R2/R3.

(3) Post-encoding visual token reduction -- Token Merging / ToMe [21] and visual-token pruning. WHAT: merge or drop redundant visual tokens AFTER encoding to save compute. DELTA: token reduction cannot ADD resolution to a region that was encoded coarsely -- it can only remove what is already there. LASA acts on the INPUT side (which regions get encoded finely), recovering signal that no post-hoc merging can recreate. These map to a budget-savings knob orthogonal to LASA (they compress R2/R3's tokens; LASA re-places them).

(4) Content-aware image resizing / saliency cropping in vision -- seam carving [20], foveated and saliency-crop pipelines. WHAT: warp or crop pixels by a saliency/energy map for display or for a single crop. DELTA: these target DISPLAY or a single region-of-interest with NO MLLM token budget, NO grounding-coordinate frame, and NO reduction to a tiling baseline; they also lack the UI domain's free, reliable element signal. LASA borrows the seam intuition [20] but recasts it as a calibrated token-budget allocation with an exact-collapse identity and a grounding rail -- it is the missing R4/R5/R6 structure these methods never needed.

(5) GUI grounding agents and benchmarks -- SeeClick / ScreenSpot [11], ScreenSpot-Pro [12], CogAgent [10], Pix2Struct-for-actions and AITW/Mind2Web pipelines [18]. WHAT: train or benchmark click/box prediction on screens, often noting that small high-resolution targets are the bottleneck [12]. DELTA: they MEASURE the small-target failure but treat tiling as fixed infrastructure; none re-places the partition to fix it. LASA is a drop-in upstream of any of these graders and is evaluated ON ScreenSpot-Pro [12] as its primary stress test (R6 supplies the ceiling).

THE TWO BARRIERS THAT KEPT IT UNSOLVED. (a) It LOOKED like changing the partition would break grounding: non-uniform tiles seem to scramble coordinates, so the safe move was to keep tiles uniform. The UNLOCK is the coordinate-frame RAIL (per-tile (o_T, s_T) decoded into one global frame) plus the EXACT-COLLAPSE check, which together make non-uniform tiling coordinate-safe and provably non-regressive at the identity default. (b) The signal looked EXPENSIVE or external: choosing where to cut by content seemed to need a separate layout model. The UNLOCK is that UI uniquely provides the layout FOR FREE -- the element boxes that BUILD the labels, the thumbnail the model already encodes, and the Android view hierarchy [13] -- so rho needs only a tiny probe to calibrate, no new labels. The tiling community optimized resolution SCALE; the graphics community optimized display; neither connected a free UI layout field to the MLLM partition under an exact-collapse guarantee.

ONE CLEAN NOVELTY SENTENCE. LASA is the first method to model a UI MLLM's sub-image partition as a calibrated, free-to-estimate per-screen layout-importance field rho(x,y) and to act on it with BOTH a seam-placement knob and a resolution-budget knob, while provably collapsing to geometric any-resolution tiling at the identity default (E_collapse = 0) and keeping grounding coordinates consistent under non-uniform tiles.

STRONGEST PRIOR-ART RISK (flagged honestly). The most dangerous neighbor is family (2): a reviewer may argue that "adaptive cropping already adapts the grid," so LASA is a reskin. LASA's defensible deltas are three and concrete: (i) CONTENT vs GEOMETRY -- adaptive cropping conditions on the screen's OUTER aspect ratio and still cuts uniform tiles; LASA conditions on the INTERNAL layout field rho and moves cuts onto gutters, which is what reduces frag_rate (a metric prior work does not even report); (ii) TWO-KNOB UNIFICATION -- the SAME field drives BOTH seam placement AND non-uniform budget, whereas prior tiling is uniform-budget by construction; (iii) EXACT COLLAPSE + RAIL -- a pixel/token-exact strict generalization with a grounding-coordinate rail, which no cropping pipeline provides. The novelty is not "tile adaptively" -- it is MEASURING layout as a calibrated field and ACTING on it with two unified knobs under a provable, grounding-safe collapse.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD PIPELINE (end-to-end). Step 0 Encode-global: any tiling MLLM [1, 2, 4, 5] computes the global thumbnail it already uses. Step 1 Estimate rho_hat for free from e1 thumbnail saliency / edge-energy (frozen/cross-fit teacher, never the live model -- O2), e2 class-agnostic element proposals from the pipeline detector, and e3 view-hierarchy boxes when present [13], combined with per-channel reliability weights (O6). Step 2 Calibrate: rho_cal = c(rho_hat), with c(.) fit on a small held-out probe that measures per-region accuracy-vs-resolution, via isotonic/Platt [19], anchored c(const) = const (O3). Step 3 KNOB1 SEAM: a 1-D dynamic program places cuts at the valleys of rho_x, rho_y minimizing crossed element mass under tile aspect/size constraints (O5). Step 4 KNOB2 BUDGET: per-region tile counts n_R ~ integral_R rho_cal under fixed total budget B (recursive sub-gridding of dense regions). Step 5 Tag-and-encode: each tile gets (o_T, s_T); encode tiles + global; the LLM decodes all grounding in one global frame (O1). Step 6 Train/eval the backbone unchanged. Safety rails fold in: frozen/cross-fit teacher (O2), do-no-harm non-inferiority gate (epsilon), and the EXACT-COLLAPSE check (rho_hat == const => P_LASA == P_geo, E_collapse = 0).

DATASETS / BENCHMARKS (all real). Ferret-UI's own benchmark (elementary referring: OCR, icon recognition, widget classification; grounding: find-text/icon/widget, widget listing; and advanced tasks) [1]; RICO / RicoSCA for Android screens WITH view hierarchies (free rho source AND oracle layout for R6) [13]; Widget Captioning and Screen2Words for element-level referring; Spotlight tasks [14]; for grounding, SeeClick / ScreenSpot and ScreenSpot-v2 [11] and, as the PRIMARY stress test, ScreenSpot-Pro (high-resolution professional screens, tiny targets ~0.07% area) [12]; for downstream action transfer, AITW and Mind2Web [18]; and DocVQA / ChartQA / InfographicsVQA as out-of-UI transfer, since seam fragmentation also afflicts document tiling [7, 8].

BACKBONES (field is backbone-agnostic): Ferret-UI [1], Ferret-UI 2 [2], and a generic LLaVA-NeXT / InternVL AnyRes model [4, 5] to show LASA is additive on top of any tiling scheme.

BASELINES = boundary recoveries R1..R6 + a strong competitor. R1 Global-only; R2 fixed 1x2/2x1 anyres (Ferret-UI [1]) -- the named status quo and COLLAPSE TARGET; R3 uniform N-gridding (Ferret-UI 2 [2] / LLaVA-NeXT [4]); R4 seam-only; R5 budget-only; R6 oracle layout (view-hierarchy rho, the ceiling); PLUS a strong aspect-ratio-adaptive cropping competitor (UReader-style [7]) to show LASA's content-awareness beats geometry-only adaptation.

METRICS. Referring accuracy (widget classification, icon recognition, OCR exact-match and normalized edit distance); grounding accuracy (box IoU@0.5 and center/point-in-box "click" accuracy as in ScreenSpot [11, 12]); ALL reported STRATIFIED by element size x seam-adjacency x layout density (stratified is PRIMARY -- O7); frag_rate (fraction of ground-truth elements crossed by a cut -- the direct MECHANISM metric); calibration of the field (AUROC and ECE of rho_cal vs oracle element density, raw vs calibrated [19]); token budget B (held FIXED, reported with every number -- O4) and iso-token accuracy curves; wall-clock overhead vs baseline (to evidence "for free" -- O6); and E_collapse (px/token exactness, CI-gateable, required = 0).

ABLATION GRID. Orthogonal 2x2 {SEAM on/off} x {BUDGET on/off} (recovers R2/R3, R4, R5, and full LASA at the four corners); estimator channels {e1, e2, e3, and combos} x {raw rho_hat vs calibrated c(.)}; budget B sweep (iso-token Pareto frontier, endpoints recover R1 and dense N-gridding); seam-solver {geometric, greedy, DP, oracle} to expose O5; coordinate-rail {on/off} to demonstrate O1 is real, not a strawman; teacher source {live, frozen, cross-fit} to expose circularity O2.

FALSIFIER-LADDER RUNS (explicit PASS conditions). ORACLE (ceiling, R6): rho from ground-truth view-hierarchy boxes -- PASS if baseline < LASA <= oracle AND the oracle shows real headroom; FALSIFIES (estimator) if free rho_hat is no better than PLACEBO. PLACEBO (token-matched random field): same budget B, random seams/budget -- PASS if LASA > placebo beyond seed noise at equal B; FALSIFIES if the gain was merely more/finer tokens. RECOVERY (reproduces baseline): rho_hat == const -- PASS if P_LASA == P_geo tile-for-tile, E_collapse = 0, reproducing R2/R3 to the token; FALSIFIES (construction) on any deviation. LEAKAGE: probe set for c(.) disjoint from eval, rho never reads eval grounding labels, calibration split frozen -- PASS if gains survive the strict disjoint split; FALSIFIES if gains vanish when leakage is removed. DO-NO-HARM: layout-uniform screens (single full-bleed image/video) -- PASS if pooled accuracy >= baseline - epsilon on EVERY cell; FALSIFIES on any regression beyond epsilon at the identity/near-identity default.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (as HYPOTHESES; provable items flagged). H1 [conjectured->measured]: a MODEST pooled lift over geometric tiling but a LARGE lift on small-element and seam-adjacent strata at FIXED token budget -- the GRADIENT of lift across strata is itself evidence that rho captures real heterogeneity (GRIDLOCK is real). H2 [conjectured->measured]: both knobs contribute -- SEAM repairs fragmentation (grounding IoU/click up), BUDGET recovers small-element resolution (referring OCR/icon up); full LASA > R4 and > R5 individually. H3 [conjectured->measured]: frag_rate drops sharply under SEAM and its reduction CORRELATES with the grounding-accuracy gain (direct mechanism confirmation). H4 [conjectured->measured]: calibrated rho_cal achieves AUROC well above 0.5 vs oracle element density with ECE far below raw saliency (calibration is load-bearing). H5 [conjectured->measured]: free rho_hat approaches the ORACLE (R6) ceiling and clearly beats the token-matched PLACEBO. H6 [conjectured->measured]: LASA reaches baseline accuracy at FEWER tokens -- an iso-accuracy efficiency win whose Pareto frontier dominates geometric tiling. P1 [provable-by-construction]: EXACT-COLLAPSE -- rho_hat == const => P_LASA == P_geo, E_collapse = 0 (zero regression at identity). P2 [provable-by-construction]: boundary recoveries R1..R6 are exact special points of the field. P3 [provable-by-construction underwriting a conjectured->measured outcome]: DO-NO-HARM non-inferiority within epsilon at the identity/near-identity default, underwritten by P1; P1 is exact, while empirical near-identity behavior under an estimated field is a separate, measured claim.

Responsible-AI and Deployment Considerations. (1) Privacy of re-allocated resolution: UI screens contain PII, and the Ferret-UI line already obfuscates personal content [1]; because BUDGET preferentially up-resolves dense text regions, LASA could inadvertently sharpen password fields, messages, or personal data. We therefore (a) support a privacy-aware rho that does NOT preferentially up-sample regions flagged sensitive by the view hierarchy (secure-text fields are labeled on Android/iOS), and (b) report whether LASA changes measured PII exposure relative to baseline. (2) Accessibility framing: the intro motivates UI understanding by accessibility [1]; LASA's small-element gains should be evaluated specifically on accessibility-relevant elements (tiny toggles, low-contrast icons), and improvements there are the intended positive impact. (3) Calibration governance: c(.) is fit on a disjoint probe; we report ECE separately per platform (iPhone/Android/iPad/web/TV) because a field calibrated on one platform's layout conventions can mis-rank another's, and we require a minimum probe size before deployment. (4) Coordinate-frame safety: since downstream agents convert boxes into taps, the (o_T, s_T) rail and the E_collapse = 0 check are reported as correctness gates, and LASA is positioned as DECISION-SUPPORT for grounding, not an autonomous tapping safety guarantee -- high-risk actions still require a human/verifier in the loop. (5) Evaluation honesty: token budget B is a REQUIRED (not optional) reporting item alongside every accuracy number, all outcomes H1-H6 are explicitly HYPOTHESES (the provable EXACT-COLLAPSE identity does not imply empirical gains), and generalization is scoped strictly to the tested platforms, resolutions, and scripts -- right-to-left layouts, non-Latin OCR, animated/transient UIs, and game canvases are outside the validated scope. (6) Asset licensing: RICO [13] and screenshot corpora carry dataset-specific terms, which we enumerate in an assets section, and we release the probe set (with per-region resolution-sweep outcomes) so c(.) is independently verifiable.
